Parole eligibility offers a potential pathway to reentry. Explore how Indiana's prison system functions, and understand the disparities in release decisions.

:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Note: Parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with original sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Total prison population pulled from BJS Prisoners data (2010-2020), released from prison to parole pulled from Annual Parole Survey (2010-2018), eligible for parole pulled from NCRP data (2010-2020).

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 655 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 2% of the prison population.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 65% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 92% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 41% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 2-4.9 years, accounting for 36% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to property, accounting for 27% of this group.


Note: The parole-eligible prison population only includes people incarcerated for a new crime and with sentence lengths between 1-25 years. Source: NCRP (2020)
``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::
